docs.title: Sabs' Math Notes

docs.text:
Those of you who are taking MAT102 currently or in the future may be struggling with how to prove a function is surjective. I've made this guide in an effort to hopefully reduce that anxiety and help you actually understand the concept of a surjective function. The definition of surjectivity is: \[ 	ext{If we have a function } f: A 	o B, 	ext{ then } B \subseteq f(A). \] By the definition of what it means to be a subset , this means that every element of B is an element of f(A) . Using quantifiers , this can be written as: \[  orall z \, (z \in B \implies z \in f(A)). \] More rigorously, surjectivity means: \[  orall y \in B, \, \exists x \in A 	ext{ such that } f(x) = y. \] The most common way to prove this is to use the following proof structure: Let \(y \in B\) . Let \(x =\) "an expression in terms of \(y\) " such that \(x \in A\) (so that \(x\) is a valid input for \(f\) ). In most cases, a good choice is setting \(x\) to the "inverse" of the function. Show that \(f(x) = y\) when plugged in. These steps may be confusing to understand, so here's a simple example: \[ 	ext{Let } f: \mathbb{R} 	o [0, \infty), \quad f(x) = x^2. 	ext{ Prove that } f 	ext{ is surjective.} \] Proof: Let \(y \in [0, \infty)\) . Since \(y \geq 0\) , we know that \(\sqrt{y} \in \mathbb{R}\) (no undefined/imaginary value in the domain/input of \(f\) ), so let \(x = \sqrt{y}\) . \[ f(x) = (\sqrt{y})^2 = y. \] Thus, for all \(y \in [0, \infty)\) , there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \(f(x) = y\) . Therefore, \(f\) is surjective. QED. To disprove surjectivity, it is enough to show that there exists an element of B that is not in the image of \(f\) . In quantifiers, this is the negation of the above statement: \[ 
eg \left(  orall y \in B, \, \exists x \in A 	ext{ such that } f(x) = y ight). \] Which is equivalent to: \[ \exists y \in B 	ext{ such that }  orall x \in A, \, f(x) 
eq y. \] If you can prove this, then \(f\) is not surjective. Intuitively, this means: pick a value in the codomain that the function never outputs. To make things clearer, here's a simple example: \[ 	ext{Let } f: \mathbb{Z} 	o \mathbb{Z}, \quad f(x) = 2x. 	ext{ Prove that } f 	ext{ is not surjective.} \] Proof: Let \(y = 1\) so that \(y \in \mathbb{Z}\) . For any \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\) , \(f(x) = 2x\) is always even , so there is no \(x\) such that \(f(x) = 1 = y\) , since \(1\) is odd . Thus, there exists \(y \in \mathbb{Z}\) such that for all \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\) , \(f(x) 
eq y\) . Therefore, \(f\) is not surjective. QED. Note: You may realize that \(f(A) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in A \}\) is just the image (range) of the function by definition, so another way to define surjectivity is to say \(f(A) = B\) . Since \(B \subseteq f(A)\) and \(f(A) \subseteq B\) , but because the image is always a subset of the codomain of a function, \(f(A) \subseteq B\) is trivial, so we really only need to prove \(B \subseteq f(A)\) . This is just a general approach, and not every question will be solvable by this exact approach. A single algorithm does not exist that works for all surjectivity proofs. Sometimes you will have to use your head more and work with the existing definitions you have. As a wise man once said, "Definition. Definition is always your friend."
